message.content:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Passing tests: 7
Recent Green phase: Added special-case branch — if items.length === 3 and all runes, return COMPONENT_BLOCK_PREMIUM (60); otherwise sum BASE_PREMIUM_BY_TYPE.

Refactor the implementation while keeping all tests green. Run `pnpm test` to confirm.

Note: upcoming tests include "4 runes → 100 G (no block)", "7 runes → 175 G (block of 3 + 4 singles)", "2 runes + 1 moonstone → 75 G (no block, different types)", "3 runes + 3 moonstones → 120 G (two separate blocks)". So blocks ultimately need to group alike components and apply per-group-of-3 discount. The current code only handles exactly 3-alike case — don't over-generalize yet; just clean up what's there.

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, return after completion.

attachment.content:
- red: TDD Red Phase
- green: TDD Green Phase
- test-list: TDD Test List Phase
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
7 passing. Now let me analyze the current code for refactoring opportunities.

## Step 1: Naming Evaluation

- `basePremium(items)` - clearly reveals intent (base premium for items). Keep.
- `percentOf(amount, percent)` - clear. Keep.
- `firstInsuranceSurcharge(base)` - clear. Keep.
- `quotePremium(items)` - clear. Keep.
- `executeStep` - clear given the step shape. Keep.
- `COMPONENT_BLOCK_SIZE`, `COMPONENT_BLOCK_PREMIUM` - clear constants. Keep.

The naming is solid. The local parameter `i` in `items.every((i) => i.type === "rune")` could be renamed to `item` for consistency with the rest of the file (it uses `item` in the reduce).

## Step 2: APP Mass Calculation (current `basePremium`)

```typescript
const basePremium = (items: Item[]): number => {
  if (items.length === COMPONENT_BLOCK_SIZE && items.every((i) => i.type === "rune")) {
    return COMPONENT_BLOCK_PREMIUM;
  }
  return items.reduce((sum, item) => sum + BASE_PREMIUM_BY_TYPE[item.type], 0);
};
```

Components:
- Constants: 1 ("rune") = 1
- Bindings: 7 (items, i, i.type, sum, item, item.type, COMPONENT_BLOCK_PREMIUM ref) ≈ 7
- Invocations: 5 (length access, every, reduce, +, BASE_PREMIUM_BY_TYPE lookup) ≈ 10
- Conditionals: 1 (if) = 4
- Loops: 2 (every, reduce) = 10

Mass ≈ 32

## Step 3: Apply Simple Design Rules

**Rule 2 (Reveals Intent)**: The condition `items.length === 3 && items.every(i => i.type === "rune")` somewhat obscures that this is "a block of 3 alike runes". Extracting a named helper `isBlockOfRunes` would reveal intent more clearly. The hint says "don't over-generalize yet" — so I'll keep it specific to the current behavior but make it more readable.

**Rule 3 (No Duplication)**: No duplication.

**Rule 4 (Fewest Elements)**: Adding a helper adds an element, but improves clarity (Rule 2 trumps).

I'll make a small refactoring: extract the block detection into a clearly-named helper and rename `i` to `item` for consistency.

content.text:
All 7 tests still passing.

## Step 5: APP Mass Calculation After Refactoring

```typescript
const isBlockOfAlikeRunes = (items: Item[]): boolean =>
  items.length === COMPONENT_BLOCK_SIZE && items.every((item) => item.type === "rune");

const sumOfBasePremiums = (items: Item[]): number =>
  items.reduce((sum, item) => sum + BASE_PREMIUM_BY_TYPE[item.type], 0);

const basePremium = (items: Item[]): number =>
  isBlockOfAlikeRunes(items) ? COMPONENT_BLOCK_PREMIUM : sumOfBasePremiums(items);
```

Components total:
- Constants: 1 ("rune") = 1
- Bindings: ~9 = 9
- Invocations: ~7 (length, every, reduce, +, lookup, isBlockOfAlikeRunes, sumOfBasePremiums) = 14
- Conditionals: 1 (ternary) = 4
- Loops: 2 (every, reduce) = 10

Mass ≈ 38

## Step 6: Document Decision

**Refactoring Applied**:
- Extracted `isBlockOfAlikeRunes(items)` predicate — makes the special-case rule self-documenting (Rule 2: Reveals Intent)
- Extracted `sumOfBasePremiums(items)` — names the default calculation explicitly (Rule 2)
- Replaced `if`/`return` with a ternary inside a single-expression `basePremium` — the function now reads as "either it's a block of alike runes (worth the block premium) or it's the sum of base premiums"
- Renamed `i` to `item` for consistency with the rest of the file (Rule 2)

**Mass Change**: ~32 to ~38 (slight increase due to extra invocations from helper extraction)

**Rule 2 trumps APP**: The mass went up slightly, but the code now expresses the business rule directly. When the upcoming tests force the block logic to generalize (grouping alike components, per-group-of-3 discounts across multiple types), `isBlockOfAlikeRunes` and `sumOfBasePremiums` give us clean seams to evolve — `isBlockOfAlikeRunes` will become a more general grouping function, and `sumOfBasePremiums` stays as the per-item fallback.

**Tests**: All 7 passing.

## Refactor Phase Complete
